section.title: The Sins of Eli's Sons
section.sectionRationale: Against the rising figure of the boy Samuel, the narrator sets the rotting priesthood of Eli's house. Hophni and Phinehas despise the LORD's offering and defile His sanctuary; Eli rebukes them feebly but honors his sons above God. A man of God then pronounces judgment: this priestly house will be cut off, both sons will die in one day, and God will raise up a faithful priest who serves before His anointed forever. It is one movement: the proud house falls while God prepares a faithful one.
section.thesis: The LORD who exalts the lowly also brings down the proud: Eli's sons treat His holy offering with contempt and honor themselves above Him, so their house is doomed, while in their place God promises to raise up a faithful priest who does His will and walks before His anointed, a promise that reaches beyond Samuel and Zadok to Christ.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the greed and contempt of Eli's sons, verses 12 to 17; Samuel growing between two households, verses 18 to 21; Eli's weak rebuke and his sons' hardness, verses 22 to 26; and the man of God's oracle of judgment and a faithful priest, verses 27 to 36.

units.label: The greed and contempt of Eli's sons
units.note: treating the LORD's offering with contempt

verses.text: Now the sons of Eli were wicked men; they had no regard for the LORD
or for the custom of the priests with the people. When any man offered a sacrifice, the servant of the priest would come with a three-pronged meat fork while the meat was boiling

and plunge it into the pan or kettle or cauldron or cooking pot. And the priest would claim for himself whatever the meat fork brought up. This is how they treated all the Israelites who came to Shiloh.

Even before the fat was burned, the servant of the priest would come and say to the man who was sacrificing, “Give the priest some meat to roast, because he will not accept boiled meat from you, but only raw.”

And if any man said to him, “The fat must be burned first; then you may take whatever you want,” the servant would reply, “No, you must give it to me right now. If you refuse, I will take it by force!”
Thus the sin of these young men was severe in the sight of the LORD, for they were treating the LORD’s offering with contempt.

units.label: Samuel growing between two households
units.note: the boy Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD

verses.text: Now Samuel was ministering before the LORD, a boy wearing a linen ephod.
Each year his mother would make him a little robe and bring it to him when she went with her husband to offer the annual sacrifice.
And Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife, saying, “May the LORD give you children by this woman in place of the one she dedicated to the LORD.” Then they would go home.
So the LORD attended to Hannah, and she conceived and gave birth to three sons and two daughters. Meanwhile, the boy Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD.

units.label: Eli's weak rebuke and his sons' hardness
units.note: they would not listen to their father

verses.text: Now Eli was very old, and he heard about everything his sons were doing to all Israel and how they were sleeping with the women who served at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
“Why are you doing these things?” Eli said to his sons. “I hear about your wicked deeds from all these people.
No, my sons; it is not a good report I hear circulating among the LORD’s people.

If a man sins against another man, God can intercede for him; but if a man sins against the LORD, who can intercede for him?” But they would not listen to their father, since the LORD intended to put them to death.
And the boy Samuel continued to grow in stature and in favor with the LORD and with man.

units.label: Judgment and the promise of a faithful priest
units.note: I will raise up for Myself a faithful priest

verses.text: Then a man of God came to Eli and told him, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Did I not clearly reveal Myself to your father’s house when they were in Egypt under Pharaoh’s house?

And out of all the tribes of Israel I selected your father to be My priest, to offer sacrifices on My altar, to burn incense, and to wear an ephod in My presence. I also gave to the house of your father all the food offerings of the Israelites.

Why then do you kick at My sacrifice and offering that I have prescribed for My dwelling place? You have {{kabad|honored}} your sons more than Me by fattening yourselves with the best of all the offerings of My people Israel.’

Therefore, the LORD, the God of Israel, declares: ‘I did indeed say that your house and the house of your father would walk before Me forever. But now the LORD declares: Far be it from Me! For I will honor those who honor Me, but those who despise Me will be disdained.
Behold, the days are coming when I will cut off your strength and the strength of your father’s house, so that no one in it will reach old age.
You will see distress in My dwelling place. Despite all that is good in Israel, no one in your house will ever again reach old age.
And every one of you that I do not cut off from My altar, I will cause your eyes to fail and your heart to grieve. All your descendants will die by the sword of men.
And this sign shall come to you concerning your two sons Hophni and Phinehas: They will both die on the same day.

Then I will raise up for Myself a {{neeman|faithful}} priest. He will do whatever is in My heart and mind. And I will build for him an enduring house, and he will walk before My {{mashiach|anointed one}} for all time.
And everyone left in your house will come and bow down to him for a piece of silver or a morsel of bread, pleading, “Please appoint me to some priestly office so that I can eat a piece of bread.”’”

kabad.gloss: To be heavy, to honor, to give weight to. The same root runs through the charge and the verdict: Eli honored his sons more than God, and God answers, those who honor Me I will honor, but those who despise Me will be disdained. To honor God is to give Him His true weight; Eli gave that weight to his sons instead.

neeman.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, enduring, from the root aman, to be firm or reliable. God promises a neeman priest who does His heart and mind, and a neeman, enduring, house for him. In contrast to a faithless priesthood being cut off, God raises up faithfulness, a promise that reaches its fullness in Christ, the faithful High Priest.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, messiah. The faithful priest will walk before God's anointed forever. Already in chapter 2 the anointed King, first named in Hannah's song, reappears; priest and anointed King are bound together, a pairing fulfilled in Jesus, who is both the faithful Priest and the Anointed King.

basic.context: The narrator keeps switching between two pictures. On one side are Eli's sons, Hophni and Phinehas, priests who use their office to rob God and the people, seizing the best of the sacrifices by force and even defiling the sanctuary with immorality. On the other side is the boy Samuel, quietly growing up in God's presence, wearing his little robe. Eli scolds his sons weakly but will not restrain them, because he honors them above God. So a man of God arrives with a hard word: this priestly line will be cut off, both sons will die on one day, and God will raise up a faithful priest instead. The question is: what does God do with leaders who despise Him? He brings them down, and He raises up faithfulness in their place.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative with a strong moral and theological point, told by contrast: the corrupt house of Eli against the faithful child Samuel. It is not merely a warning to bad clergy; it shows God's holiness against those who treat holy things with contempt, and His faithfulness to raise up a true priest. We should feel the weight of the phrase they had no regard for the LORD, and the hope of the promised faithful priest, without turning the passage into a simple lesson on parenting.
basic.originalLanguages: The charge turns on the word kabad, to honor or give weight: Eli honored his sons more than God, and God replies, those who honor Me I will honor. In place of a faithless line God promises a neeman, a faithful and enduring, priest who does His heart and mind, and this priest will walk before God's mashiach, His anointed one. Priest and Anointed King stand together, and both meet in Christ.
basic.typology: The promised faithful priest who does all that is in God's heart points beyond the immediate fulfillments in Samuel and later Zadok to Jesus Christ, the truly faithful High Priest. The contrast between greedy shepherds who fatten themselves and the coming faithful one anticipates the Good Shepherd who lays down His life. And the pairing of faithful priest and anointed King foreshadows Christ, who is both.
basic.christ: God promises to raise up a faithful priest who will do whatever is in His heart and mind, with an enduring house, walking before His anointed forever. Samuel and later the priestly line of Zadok partly fulfill this, but the words strain beyond them. Only Jesus is the perfectly faithful Priest who always does the Father's will, whose house endures forever, and who serves as Priest before, and as, the Anointed King. Where Eli's sons treated the offering with contempt, Christ offered Himself as the one true sacrifice.
basic.god: God is holy, and He takes with deadly seriousness how His worship and His people are treated; contempt for His offering is a severe sin in His sight. He is just, cutting off a house that has despised Him even after generations of privilege. Yet He is faithful, refusing to leave His people without a true priest, and He binds His promise to His anointed one. He honors those who honor Him, and He will not be mocked.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7 presents Christ as the permanent, faithful High Priest. Ezekiel 34 condemns shepherds who feed themselves rather than the flock. Malachi 2 rebukes priests who despise God's name. And 1 Peter 2 shows how God honors the One others rejected.
basic.oneStory: The same God who lifts the lowly Samuel brings down the proud house of Eli. The doom of a contemptuous priesthood and the promise of a faithful priest before God's anointed carry the story forward, past Samuel and Zadok, to Christ, the faithful High Priest and Anointed King in whom worship is finally made pure.

context.literary: The narrator interleaves reports of Eli's wicked sons with brief notices of Samuel's growth, verses 18, 21, and 26, building a deliberate contrast between a falling house and a rising boy. This same alternating technique will structure the rise of David against the fall of Saul. The man of God's oracle in verses 27 to 36 is the theological center, interpreting the whole and pointing ahead.
context.historical: Priests were entitled to specified portions of the sacrifices, but the law fixed which parts and forbade taking the fat, which belonged to God. Eli's sons overturned this, seizing raw meat by force and taking what was God's. Sleeping with women who served at the sanctuary entrance defiled worship at its heart. Shiloh housed the Tent of Meeting and the ark before the Jerusalem temple.
context.canonical: The oracle against Eli's house begins a thread that runs to the slaughter of the priests at Nob and the removal of Abiathar under Solomon, when Zadok's line is confirmed. The promise of a faithful priest who serves before the LORD's anointed opens toward the New Testament portrait of Christ as the great High Priest, fulfilling and surpassing every priestly hope.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God's holiness against a priesthood that despises Him, to set the falling house of Eli against the rising Samuel, and to plant the promise of a faithful priest before God's anointed, a hope that reaches its fullness in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the sins of Eli's sons and God's judgment; it does not prescribe their behavior, nor is it a mere manual on discipline. What endures is God's holiness toward contempt of holy things, His justice against proud leaders, and His faithful promise of a true priest fulfilled in Jesus.

debates.question: How can the sons' refusal to listen and God's intention to put them to death fit together, verse 25?

views.label: Judicial hardening after persistent sin
views.case: The sons had long treated God with contempt; the LORD's intention to put them to death is the judicial confirmation of a hardness they themselves chose, so their refusal to listen is both their guilt and God's righteous sentence unfolding.

views.label: God's sovereignty over even the wicked
views.case: The verse states plainly that the LORD intended their death, underscoring that God's purposes stand even over rebellious hearts, without excusing the sons, who remain fully responsible for despising Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage holds human responsibility and divine sovereignty together: the sons are guilty for their contempt, and God's just purpose is being accomplished; it warns against presuming on privilege while despising God.

debates.question: Who is the faithful priest promised in verse 35?

views.label: Fulfilled in stages through Samuel and Zadok
views.case: Samuel serves faithfully at once, and the enduring priestly house is realized in Zadok's line, confirmed under Solomon when Eli's descendant Abiathar is removed; the promise finds real historical fulfillment in Israel's priesthood.

views.label: Ultimately fulfilled in Christ
views.case: The language of a priest who always does God's heart, with a house enduring forever and serving before the anointed for all time, strains beyond any Old Testament priest and is answered only in Jesus, the faithful High Priest, Hebrews 7.

debates.bearing: Both are true in sequence: the promise unfolds through Samuel and Zadok yet reaches its fullness in Christ, so that the reader looks past every faithful but mortal priest to the everlasting one.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the oracle turns on the word kabad, to be heavy, to give weight, to honor. Eli, God says, has honored his sons more than Me, giving them the weight that belongs to God alone; and the verdict answers in the same word, those who honor Me I will honor, but those who despise Me will be disdained. To honor God is simply to treat Him as weighty, as holy, as God, precisely what Eli's sons would not do when they treated the LORD's offering with contempt. Over against a priesthood being cut off, God promises a neeman priest, from aman, to be firm, trustworthy, faithful, one who will do whatever is in God's heart and mind, and He will build him a neeman, an enduring, house. And this faithful priest will walk before God's mashiach, His anointed one, the note from Hannah's song sounding again. Faithful priest and anointed King are set side by side, a pairing that will come together perfectly in Jesus, the faithful High Priest who is Himself the Christ.

typology.type: The promised faithful priest who does all God's heart
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the faithful and merciful High Priest who always does the Father's will, Hebrews 2:17 and 7:23-28.

typology.type: The greedy priests fattening themselves versus the faithful one to come
typology.fulfillment: The false shepherds of Ezekiel 34 contrasted with the Good Shepherd who lays down His life, John 10:11.

typology.type: The faithful priest walking before the anointed one forever
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who is both the everlasting Priest and the Anointed King, uniting the two offices, Hebrews 7:1-3.

study.christ: God promises to raise up for Himself a faithful priest who will do whatever is in His heart and mind, whose house will endure, and who will walk before His anointed one forever. Samuel and, later, the priestly line of Zadok partly fulfill this in Israel's history, yet the words reach far beyond any mortal priest. Only Jesus is the perfectly faithful High Priest who always does the Father's will, whose priesthood is permanent because He lives forever, Hebrews 7:24-25, and who serves as Priest before, and is Himself, the Anointed King. Where Hophni and Phinehas seized the sacrifices for themselves and treated God's offering with contempt, Christ offered Himself, the one true and sufficient sacrifice, honoring the Father where they despised Him. The falling house of Eli makes us long for the faithful Priest who cannot fall.
study.god: This passage reveals a God who is intensely holy and cannot be treated lightly. Contempt for His offering is not a minor lapse but a severe sin in His sight, and honoring self above Him invites judgment even upon a house long privileged with priestly office. He is just: He cuts off what has despised Him and keeps His warning to the letter, both sons dead in a single day. Yet He is also faithful and gracious: He will not abandon His people to faithless leaders but promises a true priest of His own raising, bound to His anointed one. He honors those who honor Him and will not be mocked, and in that severe holiness there is hope, for it guarantees a pure worship at last.

crossReferences.note: Christ is the permanent, faithful High Priest who always lives to intercede, fulfilling the promise of a faithful priest.
God condemns shepherds who feed themselves instead of the flock, as Eli's sons fattened themselves on the offerings.
The sign of verse 34 is fulfilled: Hophni and Phinehas die on the same day when the ark is captured.
The word against Eli's house is fulfilled when Solomon removes Abiathar, and Zadok's line is confirmed.

study.oneStory: The God who lifts the lowly Samuel brings down the proud house of Eli. A priesthood that despised the LORD is doomed, while God promises a faithful priest who does His heart and walks before His anointed forever. That promise moves through Samuel and Zadok and comes to rest in Christ, the faithful High Priest and Anointed King, in whom the worship that Eli's sons defiled is finally made pure and secure.

quiz.q: What made the sin of Eli's sons so severe in the LORD's sight?

options.text: As priests they treated the LORD's offering with contempt, robbing God and the people and defiling His worship.
options.note: Verse 17 names their contempt for the LORD's offering as the heart of the sin.

options.text: They took portions of the sacrifices by force before the fat was burned.
options.note: True and part of it, but the deeper charge is contempt for God and His worship, not only greed.

options.text: They failed to keep proper records of the sacrifices.
options.note: The issue was contempt and abuse, not administrative failure.

options.text: They offered too many sacrifices to the LORD.
options.note: The opposite; they abused and despised the offerings, not offered too many.

quiz.q: What does God's word to Eli, those who honor Me I will honor, reveal?

options.text: Eli gave the weight of honor to his sons that belonged to God, and God repays honor with honor and contempt with disdain.
options.note: The word kabad ties the charge and the verdict together, verses 29 to 30.

options.text: God rewards those who respect Him and judges those who despise Him.
options.note: True, but misses that the specific charge was honoring his sons above God.

options.text: God simply wanted Eli to speak more sternly to his sons.
options.note: The issue was misplaced honor, not merely a firmer tone.

options.text: God promised to honor Eli's house no matter what they did.
options.note: The opposite; the house is cut off for despising Him.

quiz.q: Who is the faithful priest God promises to raise up in verse 35?

options.text: One fulfilled partly in Samuel and Zadok but ultimately in Christ, the everlasting faithful High Priest.
options.note: The promise unfolds in history yet reaches its fullness in Jesus, Hebrews 7.

options.text: Samuel and later the priestly line of Zadok.
options.note: A true stage of fulfillment, but the language of a house enduring forever points beyond them to Christ.

options.text: Eli himself, restored after his repentance.
options.note: The promise stands against Eli's house, not for its restoration.

options.text: No one; it was an empty threat.
options.note: God really does raise up faithful priesthood, fulfilled finally in Christ.

quiz.q: How does the narrator use the boy Samuel in this passage?

options.text: He sets Samuel's quiet growth in God's favor against the corruption of Eli's sons, a rising figure against a falling house.
options.note: The alternating notices of Samuel, verses 18, 21, 26, contrast the two households.

options.text: He shows Samuel growing up faithfully at the sanctuary.
options.note: True, but the point is the deliberate contrast with the falling house of Eli.

options.text: He presents Samuel as one of Eli's wicked sons.
options.note: Samuel is the opposite of Eli's sons, not one of them.

options.text: He includes Samuel only by accident, with no purpose.
options.note: The contrast is deliberate and structural.

quiz.q: How does this passage point forward to Christ?

options.text: It promises a faithful priest who does all God's heart and serves before His anointed, fulfilled in Jesus the faithful High Priest and Anointed King.
options.note: The oracle joins faithful priest and anointed one, both met in Christ.

options.text: It contrasts greedy shepherds with a coming faithful one.
options.note: True, but leaves out that Christ is both the faithful Priest and the anointed King the promise names.

options.text: It teaches only that bad priests will be punished.
options.note: Judgment is real, but the forward-looking promise of a faithful priest is the point that leads to Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The promise of a faithful priest before the anointed clearly points to Jesus.

questions: Eli's sons treated holy things with contempt because they had no regard for the LORD. Are there ways you handle God's worship, word, or gifts casually, without giving Him His true weight?
Eli honored his sons more than God. Is there a relationship or loyalty in your life that you are quietly placing above your loyalty to the LORD?
Eli rebuked his sons but would not truly restrain them. Where might God be calling you to loving but firm action, rather than words that cost you nothing?
God promised a faithful priest even as a faithless one fell. How does it comfort you that Jesus is the faithful High Priest who always does the Father's will and never fails?
The passage says God honors those who honor Him. What would it look like this week to give God the weight and first place He deserves in your decisions?

translationNotes.note: The source has an em dash after before the LORD; rendered here as a comma, a boy wearing a linen ephod, with no change of meaning.
God can intercede for him renders a term the BSB footnote allows as the judges; the sense is that a human mediator can address wrongs between people, but sin against the LORD Himself is graver.
The verbs honor and despise translate forms of kabad and its opposite; the same root ties Eli's fault, honoring his sons, to God's verdict, honoring those who honor Him.
The BSB follows the Dead Sea Scrolls and Septuagint reading that his descendants will die by the sword; the Masoretic Text reads will die as mortals, or in the prime of life.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 2:12-36. One em dash in verse 18 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
